title: Reading wraps per inch (WPI)
subtitle: How to use a wraps-per-inch measurement to match hand-spun yarn to patterns
excerpt: Wraps per inch (WPI) is the standard measurement for comparing hand-spun yarn weight to commercial yarn categories and pattern specifications. Understanding WPI allows a spinner to test whether a finished skein will substitute for a commercial yarn in a pattern, and to set a consistent target during spinning. The same principle applies to metres per 100 g, which UK patterns more commonly specify.
sourceNotes: Wraps-per-inch measurement is a standard tool in hand-spinning instruction. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A measurement of yarn thickness: the number of times a length of yarn wraps around a 1-inch (2.5 cm) ruler without overlapping. Thicker yarn produces a lower WPI; finer yarn a higher WPI.
A standardised system for describing yarn thickness, from lace (finest) through fingering, sport, DK, aran, bulky, and super bulky. Each category corresponds to a WPI range and a standard knit gauge.

content.text: reading gives the spinner a number that can be compared directly to a

. WPI is measured in imperial units (per inch) because the standard reference tables are set in imperial, but the measurement itself is taken from a 1-inch (2.5 cm) section of a ruler and the result is a dimensionless count.

Hold the yarn in the non-dominant hand without tension (do not stretch it). Wrap it around a ruler or a dedicated WPI tool at a measured 1-inch section. Each wrap should touch the previous without overlapping or gapping. Count the wraps. Repeat twice and average the three readings.
Approximate WPI ranges for each standard weight category:

UK patterns more often specify yarn weight in metres per 100 g (m/100 g) than in WPI. To find the m/100 g of a hand-spun skein: weigh the skein in grams, measure its total length in metres, then calculate (total length / weight) x 100. A DK hand-spun typically runs 180 to 220 m per 100 g; an aran runs 80 to 120 m per 100 g. Both systems converge on the same result; WPI is faster for a quick in-progress check, m/100 g is more exact for pattern matching.

A spinner produces a 100-g skein that measures 14 WPI. This falls in the DK category (12 to 16 WPI). The skein is 190 m in total, giving 190 m/100 g. A pattern calling for DK at 200 m/100 g will work with this yarn, though the spinner should knit a tension square to confirm gauge before committing to the full project.
